title: Pediatric Bone Fragility: When to Worry and What to Do?

items.title: Why pediatric bone fragility affects lifetime fracture risk
items.text: Foundation for lifetime bone strength is set in the first two decades—each 10% (1 SD) gain in peak bone strength may halve lifetime fracture risk.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Each 10% (1 SD) gain in peak bone strength has been estimated to reduce an individual's lifetime fracture risk by 50%"

items.title: How ISCD defines pediatric osteoporosis
items.text: Diagnose with vertebral compression fracture without major trauma OR low BMD Z-score <−2 plus ≥2 long-bone fractures by age 10 or ≥3 by age 19—finger/toe fractures do not count.
items.reference: Defining Pediatric Osteoporosis: "Low bone mineral content or density for age (a standard deviation for age or Z-score less than -2) plus 2 or more long bone fractures by age 10 years or 3 or more by age 19 years"

items.title: Why DXA alone is an imperfect pediatric fracture predictor
items.text: DXA, pQCT, and HR-pQCT are imperfect surrogates—expert panel restricted osteoporosis diagnosis to significant fracture history because no pediatric FRAX equivalent exists.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "these have proven to be imperfect surrogate predictors of fracture risk."

items.title: How bisphosphonates perform in pediatric osteoporosis
items.text: Bisphosphonates reduce fractures in most primary and secondary osteoporosis causes except anorexia nervosa—none are FDA-approved in children due to limited RCTs using fracture endpoints.
items.reference: Drug Therapy: "Bisphosphonates appear to be effective in most causes of primary and secondary osteoporosis with the exception of anorexia nervosa."

items.title: How to evaluate a dancer with stress fracture and amenorrhea
items.text: Athletic triad combines energy deficit, hypogonadism, and bone stress—exclude hyperprolactinemia, POI, celiac disease, and vitamin D deficiency; DXA may not change management.
items.reference: Case 1: "The diagnosis is one of exclusion, and it is important to exclude other causes of amenorrhea, including primary ovarian insufficiency or hyperprolactinemia (Answer C)."

items.title: When recurrent childhood fractures warrant genetic testing
items.text: Femur fracture before age 5, low-trauma fractures, and family history justify OI genetic testing—even normal DXA Z-scores do not exclude OI.
items.reference: Case 2: "Genetic testing (Answer E) was performed in this patient, which confirmed a pathogenic variant in the COL1A1 gene."

items.title: Why lateral spine x-ray precedes bisphosphonates in DMD on deflazacort
items.text: Declining BMD Z-score alone is insufficient for therapy—vertebral fracture establishes osteoporosis diagnosis and indication for IV bisphosphonates; oral alendronate is less effective for vertebral fractures.
items.reference: Case 3: "Finding a vertebral fracture would establish the diagnosis of osteoporosis and would be an indication to offer bisphosphonates."

items.title: Bone health evaluation in nonambulatory children with cerebral palsy
items.text: DXA will be low due to narrow bones from absent loading—lateral spine x-ray by age 6–8 guides treatment; bisphosphonates reserved for fragility fractures.
items.reference: Case 5: "A lateral spine x-ray (Answer E) would influence treatment."

items.title: STOPP consortium: vertebral fracture cascade in glucocorticoid-treated children
items.text: Even one mild grade 1 vertebral fracture predicts future fractures in ALL and other GC-treated cohorts—71% of incident fractures occur in first 2 years.
items.reference: Case 3: "The presence of even 1 mild grade 1 vertebral fracture predicted future fractures, a phenomenon called the "vertebral fracture cascade.""

items.title: How to prepare vitamin D-deficient rickets before bisphosphonates
items.text: Replete 25-hydroxyvitamin D to >20 ng/mL before bisphosphonate infusion to reduce hypocalcemia and osteomalacia exacerbation—lateral spine x-ray still indicated.
items.reference: Case 4: "initiate bisphosphonate therapy, but only after vitamin D stores have been repleted (25-hydroxyvitamin D >20 ng/mL [>50 nmol/L]) to reduce the risk of postinfusion hypocalcemia"

items.title: Pediatric bone turnover markers in clinical practice
items.text: PINP and CTx have high variability in children—not recommended for diagnosis but may monitor oversuppression during drug therapy.
items.reference: Evaluation: "These tests are not recommended for diagnosis or decision making for treatment, but they may be useful to monitor for over-suppression of bone turnover during pharmacologic therapy."

items.title: Why teriparatide is not used in growing children
items.text: Synthetic parathyroid hormone (teriparatide) carries a black box warning against use in growing patients—bisphosphonates remain main pharmacologic option.
items.reference: Drug Therapy: "The most commonly prescribed of these, synthetic parathyroid hormone, carries a black box warning against its use in growing patients."

items.question: A 14-year-old ballet dancer with stress fracture, amenorrhea, and low BMI. Best initial evaluation?
options: DXA whole body and spine only
Prolactin, LH, FSH, estradiol, 25-OH vitamin D, and celiac screen
items.explanation: Athletic triad workup excludes other amenorrhea causes and checks vitamin D/celiac before imaging decisions.
items.reference: Case 1: "Answer: C) Prolactin, LH, FSH, estradiol, and 25-hydroxyvitamin D measurements and celiac screen"

items.question: A 14-year-old boy with 7 long-bone fractures including femur at age 3. Best evaluation now?
items.explanation: Concerning fracture pattern warrants COL1A1/COL1A2 testing—confirmed OI in case.
items.reference: Case 2: "Answer: E) Genetic studies for osteogenesis imperfecta"

items.question: A 9-year-old with DMD on deflazacort has spine BMD Z-score declining to −2.1 without fractures. Best advice?
options: Repeat DXA in 6 months and treat if Z < −2.5
items.explanation: Vertebral imaging determines osteoporosis diagnosis—BMD change alone is insufficient.
items.reference: Case 3: "Answer: D) Obtain lateral spine x-ray"

items.question: A nonambulatory 7-year-old with epilepsy on ketogenic diet requests bone density testing. Best study?
options: DXA distal femur, spine, and total body
items.explanation: Immobilization lowers DXA predictively; spine x-ray detects vertebral fractures that change management.
items.reference: Case 5: "Answer: E) Lateral spine x ray"

items.question: Pediatric osteoporosis by ISCD can be diagnosed with:
options: DXA Z-score −1.5 and one wrist fracture
Vertebral compression fracture without major trauma
items.explanation: Vertebral compression fracture alone suffices; long-bone criteria exclude digits.
items.reference: Defining Pediatric Osteoporosis: "One or more vertebral compression fractures occurring without local disease or major trauma"

items.question: By age 16, approximate fraction of healthy boys with at least one fracture is:
items.explanation: Up to 64% of boys and 40% of girls have fractured by 16—context for interpreting recurrence.
items.reference: Case 2: "By 16 years of age, up to 40% of otherwise healthy girls and 64% of boys will have experienced at least 1 broken bone"

items.question: If DXA is performed in athletic triad evaluation, preferred regions are:
items.explanation: Case 1 specifies whole body and spine, not hip.
items.reference: Case 1: "the preferred regions of interest would be whole body and spine (Answer B) and not spine and hip (Answer A)."

items.question: Bisphosphonates in pediatric osteoporosis are LEAST effective in:
items.explanation: Explicit exception in drug therapy section.
items.reference: Drug Therapy: "with the exception of anorexia nervosa."

items.question: Initial laboratory evaluation for pediatric bone fragility should include:
options: 25-hydroxyvitamin D, calcium, phosphate, creatinine, PTH, and celiac antibodies
items.explanation: Baseline panel listed in evaluation section.
items.reference: Evaluation: "Baseline laboratory studies include measurement of serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D, calcium, phosphate, creatinine, PTH, antibodies for celiac disease, and urinary calcium excretion."

items.question: In glucocorticoid-treated DMD, oral alendronate compared with IV agents:
options: Is preferred for vertebral fracture prevention
Has not been shown as effective as IV agents for vertebral fractures in children
items.explanation: Oral alendronate less effective than IV for vertebral fractures in children on GCs.
items.reference: Case 3: "oral bisphosphonates such as alendronate have not been shown to be effective as intravenous agents in preventing vertebral fractures in children."

items.question: A infant with COL1A1 variant and hypotonia. Before starting bisphosphonates:
options: Replete vitamin D and obtain lateral spine x-ray
items.explanation: Rule out rickets and vertebral fractures; replete vitamin D before infusion.
items.reference: Case 4: "order a chemistry panel and 25-hydroxyvitamin D measurement and obtain lateral spine x-ray (Answer D)."

items.question: Concerning fracture history features in children include:
options: Distal radius fracture from fall off bicycle only
items.explanation: Low-trauma femur, hip, spine fractures suggest fragility; digits excluded from ISCD counts.
items.reference: Evaluation: "Features of a concerning fracture history include low-trauma injuries (from standing height or less) or vertebral or femur fractures"

items.question: First-line treatment for bone health in hypothalamic amenorrhea emphasizes:
options: Nutritional rehabilitation and reducing excessive exercise
items.explanation: Energy deficit drives bone fragility—nutrition first; sex steroids/IGF-1 if rehabilitation fails.
items.reference: Drug Therapy: "Nutritional therapy is recommended as first-line treatment with reduction in excessive activity if present."

items.statement: A 1 SD increase in peak bone strength may reduce lifetime fracture risk by about 50%.
items.explanation: Main conclusions cite this estimate.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Each 10% (1 SD) gain in peak bone strength has been estimated to reduce an individual's lifetime fracture risk by 50%"

items.statement: Finger and toe fractures count toward ISCD long-bone fracture criteria.
items.explanation: Guidelines emphasize long bone—not finger/toe—fractures.
items.reference: Defining Pediatric Osteoporosis: "Fractures of long bones but not fingers and toes are important in decision making about treatment."

items.statement: There is an established pediatric DXA fracture threshold equivalent to adult FRAX.
items.explanation: No pediatric FRAX-like tool exists.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "there is no tool comparable to FRAX used in adults to weight the clinical factors that aid in calculating fracture risk."

items.statement: A child with one prior fracture has approximately double the risk of a second fracture.
items.explanation: Epidemiologic data cited in case 2.
items.reference: Case 2: "The risk of a second fracture has been estimated to double for a child who has already had 1 fracture."

items.statement: Declining BMD Z-score alone in DMD on glucocorticoids is sufficient to start bisphosphonates.
items.explanation: Need vertebral fracture or ISCD fracture criteria—not BMD alone.
items.reference: Case 3: "the change in BMD Z-score alone is not a sufficient indication for drug therapy (thus, Answer E is incorrect)."

items.statement: Teriparatide is approved for routine use in growing children with osteoporosis.
items.explanation: Black box warning against use in growing patients.
items.reference: Drug Therapy: "carries a black box warning against its use in growing patients."

items.statement: DXA BMD is often low in immobilized children primarily because of smaller, narrower bones.
items.explanation: Absent skeletal loading reduces bone geometry seen on DXA.
items.reference: Case 5: "Two-dimensional assessments of BMD by DXA will be low for age as a result of the smaller, narrower bones."

items.statement: In STOPP ALL cohort, majority of incident fractures occurred after the first 2 years of therapy.
items.explanation: 71% occurred in first 2 years.
items.reference: Case 3: "71% occurred in the first 2 years of treatment."

items.assertion: Assertion: ISCD restricts pediatric osteoporosis diagnosis to significant fracture history criteria.
items.reason: Reason: DXA and CT surrogates are imperfect predictors of fracture risk in children.
items.explanation: Both true—explains restrictive criteria.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "the diagnosis of pediatric osteoporosis be restricted to those with a significant fracture history."

items.assertion: Assertion: Athletic triad evaluation should exclude hyperprolactinemia and celiac disease.
items.reason: Reason: Athletic triad is a diagnosis of exclusion.
items.explanation: Both true—case 1 workup.
items.reference: Case 1: "The diagnosis is one of exclusion, and it is important to exclude other causes of amenorrhea"

items.assertion: Assertion: Multiple low-trauma fractures including femur in childhood warrant OI genetic testing.
items.reason: Reason: Most childhood fractures are evenly distributed and never recur.
items.explanation: Assertion true; some children fracture repeatedly—reason false.
items.reference: Case 2: "some children fracture more than once, while others escape injury."

items.assertion: Assertion: Lateral spine x-ray is indicated before starting bisphosphonates in glucocorticoid-treated DMD with falling BMD Z-score.
items.reason: Reason: BMD Z-score decline alone establishes osteoporosis diagnosis.
items.explanation: Assertion true; BMD alone insufficient—reason false.
items.reference: Case 3: "the change in BMD Z-score alone is not a sufficient indication for drug therapy"

items.assertion: Assertion: Bisphosphonates reduce fractures in most pediatric primary and secondary osteoporosis.
items.reason: Reason: Bisphosphonates are equally effective in anorexia nervosa.
items.explanation: Assertion true; anorexia is the exception—reason false.
items.reference: Drug Therapy: "Bisphosphonates appear to be effective in most causes of primary and secondary osteoporosis with the exception of anorexia nervosa."

items.assertion: Assertion: Vitamin D should be repleted before bisphosphonate infusion in rickets.
items.reason: Reason: Post-infusion hypocalcemia risk rises if vitamin D is deficient.
items.explanation: Both true—case 4 protocol.
items.reference: Case 4: "only after vitamin D stores have been repleted (25-hydroxyvitamin D >20 ng/mL [>50 nmol/L]) to reduce the risk of postinfusion hypocalcemia"

items.assertion: Assertion: Lateral spine x-ray influences treatment in nonambulatory children more than DXA alone.
items.reason: Reason: DXA low values in immobilization always mandate bisphosphonates.
items.explanation: Assertion true; DXA alone does not guide treatment—reason false.
items.reference: Case 5: "finding "low bone density for age" will most likely add to parental concern but not necessarily guide treatment."

items.assertion: Assertion: A single mild vertebral fracture predicts future fractures in glucocorticoid-treated children.
items.reason: Reason: Vertebral fractures never occur in the first 2 years of ALL therapy.
items.explanation: Assertion true (vertebral fracture cascade); most fractures occur early—reason false.
items.reference: Case 3: "The presence of even 1 mild grade 1 vertebral fracture predicted future fractures, a phenomenon called the "vertebral fracture cascade.""
